elements.sent-2411-003: The theoretical development of quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) methods for uncertainty quantification of partial differential equations (PDEs) is typically centered around simplified model problems such as elliptic PDEs subject to homogeneous zero Dirichlet boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we present a theoretical treatment of the application of randomly shifted rank-1 lattice rules to electrical impedance tomography (EIT).
elements.sent-2411-005: EIT is an imaging modality, where the goal is to reconstruct the interior conductivity of an object based on electrode measurements of current and voltage taken at the boundary of the object.
elements.sent-2411-006: This is an inverse problem, which we tackle using the Bayesian statistical inversion paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-007: As the reconstruction, we consider QMC integration to approximate the unknown conductivity given current and voltage measurements.
elements.sent-2411-008: We prove under moderate assumptions placed on the parameterization of the unknown conductivity that the QMC approximation of the reconstructed estimate has a dimension-independent, faster-than-Monte Carlo cubature convergence rate.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we present numerical results for examples computed using simulated measurement data.
elements.sent-2411-010: Quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) methods are a class of high-dimensional cubature rules with equal cubature weights.
elements.sent-2411-011: QMC methods have become a popular tool in the numerical treatment of uncertainties in partial differential equation (PDE) models with random or uncertain inputs.
elements.sent-2411-012: Studied topics include elliptic eigenvalue problems~\cite{chernovle2, GGKSS2019 , optimal control~\cite{guth21,guth22 , various diffusion problems~\cite{chernovle1,kuonuyenssurvey,log5 , parametric operator equations~\cite{dicklegiaschwab,spodpaper14 as well as elliptic PDEs with random or lognormal coefficients~\cite{ghs18,log,log2,log3,log4,kks20,kss12,kssmultilevel .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: A common and sought-after advantage of these applications are the faster-than-Monte Carlo convergence rates of QMC methods and---under some moderate conditions---this convergence can be shown to be independent of the dimensionality of the associated integration problems.
elements.sent-2411-014: QMC methods are particularly well-suited to large-scale uncertainty quantification problems since it is typically easy to parallelize the computation over QMC point sets.
elements.sent-2411-015: The overwhelming majority of QMC analyses for PDEs have been carried out within the context of the forward uncertainty quantification problem of approximating the expected value \begin{equation}\mathbb E[G(u)]=\int_{\Omega}G(u(\cdot,\omega))\,\mathbb P({\rm d}\omega),\end{equation} where $(\Omega,\mathcal A,\mathbb P)$ is a probability space and $G$ is a bounded linear functional acting on the solution $u$ of an elliptic PDE with a random diffusion coefficient $a$ such as \begin{equation} -\nabla\cdot (a(\bsx,\omega)\nabla u(\bsx,\omega))=f(\bsx), \bsx\in D,~\omega\in \Omega, u(\bsx,\omega)=0, \bsx\in\partial D,~\omega\in\Omega, \label{eq:modelproblempde}\end{equation} where $f\!:D\to\mathbb R$ is a fixed source term.
elements.sent-2411-016: For mathematical analysis, the boundary conditions are typically taken to be extremely simple such as the homogeneous zero Dirichlet boundary condition in~\eqref{eq:modelproblempde , which is an unrealistic modeling assumption for most practical applications.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In addition, there have been some studies on Bayesian inversion governed by parametric PDEs \cite{dglgs19,gantnerphd,gantnerpeters,hks21,scheichlbayes,ssw20 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, these studies generally restrict their attention to simplified model problems such as~\eqref{eq:modelproblempde .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Meanwhile, electrical impedance tomography (EIT) is an imaging modality, which uses measurements of voltage and current taken over an array of electrodes placed on the boundary of an object, with the goal of reconstructing the unknown conductivity inside the object; see for example~\cite{adler2021electrical,borcea .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The most accurate way to model the measurements of EIT is to employ the complete electrode model (CEM), which accounts for the electrode shapes and contact resistances caused by resistive layers at electrode-object interfaces~\cite{cheng,hyvonen,somersalo .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The mathematical model of CEM is governed by an elliptic PDE---albeit one with more intricate boundary conditions than the problem~\eqref{eq:modelproblempde ---which suggests that one can apply QMC theory for both forward and inverse uncertainty quantification for this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, this kind of analysis does not appear to have been considered in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-023: It is the goal of this paper to rectify this situation.
elements.sent-2411-024: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-025: We briefly review basic multi-index notation in Section~\ref{sec:notations .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We define the problem setting---the so-called parametric complete electrode model---in Section~\ref{sec:pcem .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The parametric regularity analysis for both the forward model and inverse problem, i.e., the conditional mean estimator of the unknown conductivity, is carried out in Section~\ref{sec:parametricanalysis .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The basics of randomly shifted rank-1 lattice rules are discussed in Section~\ref{sec:qmc , and the error analysis for the EIT problem is presented in Section~\ref{sec:error .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Numerical experiments are showcased in Section~\ref{sec:numex .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, some conclusions and future prospects are given in Section~\ref{sec:conclusions .}

elements.sent-2411-216: For the reconstruction of the target conductivity (``ground truth''), we use the parameterization \begin{equation}\label{eq:randoma1} a(\bsx, \bsy) = \exp{\biggl(\sum_{j=1}^{20} y_j \psi_j(\bsx)\biggr)},\quad \bsx\in D,~\bsy\in (-1/2,1/2)^{20},\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\label{eq:randoma2} \psi_j(\bsx):=\frac{5}{(k_j^2+\ell_j^2)^{\vartheta}}\sin(\tfrac{1}{14}\pi k_jx_1)\sin(\tfrac{1}{14}\pi \ell_jx_2),\quad\vartheta>1,\end{equation} where the sequence $(k_j,\ell_j)_{j\ge 1}$ is an ordering of the elements of $\mathbb N\times \mathbb N$ such that $\|\psi_1\|_{L^\infty(D)}\geq \|\psi_2\|_{L^\infty(D)}\geq \cdots$.
elements.sent-2411-217: We consider two different target conductivities.
elements.sent-2411-218: The electrode measurements for the first experiment correspond to a target conductivity that is a realization of the parametric model~\eqref{eq:randoma1 --\eqref{eq:randoma2 with $\vartheta=2$ using a randomly generated vector $\bsy\sim\mathcal U((-1/2,1/2)^{20 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: For the second experiment, the ground truth corresponds to the piecewise constant target conductivity $\bsx\mapsto 1+0.2\,\chi_{\|\bsx+[4,5]^{\rm T}\|\leq 3 (\bsx)$.}
elements.sent-2411-220: In both experiments, the voltage measurements were simulated by solving the CEM forward model using a first order finite element discretization with maximum mesh diameter $h=0.748$.
elements.sent-2411-221: The computational inversion used the parametric model~\eqref{eq:randoma1 --\eqref{eq:randoma2 with $\vartheta=2$ in the first experiment and $\vartheta=1.3$ in the second experiment.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: An advantage of working in the Bayesian framework---in contrast to deterministic reconstruction algorithms such as the D-bar method~\cite{mueller2020d ---is that it is possible to estimate the uncertainty in $\widehat a$ via Bayesian credible envelopes.}
elements.sent-2411-223: To this end, we have estimated the 95\% credible envelopes for the reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-224: A conservative estimate for the $95\%$ credible envelope is $\mathbb E[\widehat a]\pm 4.47214\,\sqrt{{\rm Var (\widehat a)}$ by Chebyshev's inequality.}
elements.sent-2411-225: We computed the variance appearing in this expression using QMC pointwise in $\bsx\in D$ with $n=2^{20}$ lattice points.
elements.sent-2411-226: The obtained credible margins $4.47214\,\sqrt{{\rm Var (\widehat a)}$ are displayed in Figure~\ref{fig:cred .}}
elements.sent-2411-227: To this end, we computed the value of $\widehat a$ for $n=2^\ell$, $\ell=10,\ldots,20$, and report the approximated root-mean-square (R.M.S.) errors \begin{equation}\sqrt{\frac{1}{R(R-1)}\sum_{r'=1}^R\bigg\|\frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^R \frac{Z_n'(\cdot,\boldsymbol\delta,\boldsymbol\Delta^{(r)})}{Z_n(\boldsymbol\delta,\boldsymbol\Delta^{(r)})}-\frac{Z_n'(\cdot,\boldsymbol\delta,\boldsymbol\Delta^{(r')})}{Z_n(\boldsymbol\delta,\boldsymbol\Delta^{(r')})}\bigg\|_{L^\infty(D)}^2},\end{equation} where we used $R=16$ random shifts $\boldsymbol\Delta^{(r)}\overset{\rm i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-228: We compare the performance of the QMC method to that of a Monte Carlo (MC) method.
elements.sent-2411-229: In the case of the MC method, the value of $\mathbb E[\widehat a]$ was obtained as a sample average over a random sample $\bsy\sim \mathcal U((-1/2,1/2)^s)$ of size $n=2^{\ell}$, $\ell=10,\ldots,20$.
elements.sent-2411-230: The corresponding R.M.S.~error was obtained by averaging these values over $16$ trials.
elements.sent-2411-231: The results are displayed in Figure~\ref{fig:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-232: As expected, we observe the MC convergence rates $n^{-0.496}$ and $n^{-0.510}$ in Experiments 1 and 2, respectively, which are both extremely close to the theoretically expected MC converges rate $n^{-1/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-233: Meanwhile, the QMC method displays faster-than-MC cubature convergence rates in both experiments.
elements.sent-2411-234: In the case of the smooth target conductivity in Experiment 1, we observe an empirical convergence rate of $n^{-0.768}$ while in the case of the discontinuous conductivity in Experiment 2 we observe the empirical rate $n^{-0.603}$.
elements.sent-2411-235: No new data were created or analysed in this study.
